title: Area & Perimeter
blurb: Areas of parallelograms, triangles, trapezoids, rhombi and kites; regular polygons via the apothem; circles and sectors; and composite and shaded figures.
buildsOn: Quadrilateral properties (Unit 6), the apothem's right triangle (Unit 8), and circle measures (Unit 9); plus area/perimeter from Grade 6.

lessons.title: Area of Parallelograms & Triangles
lessons.concreteComparison: To carpet a room you need its **area**; to fence it you need its **perimeter**. Area counts the square units inside. A parallelogram is just a rectangle pushed over — same base, same height, same area — and a triangle is exactly half of one.
lessons.objective: Find the area of a parallelogram (base × height) and a triangle (½ × base × height), using the perpendicular height rather than a slanted side.
concept: **Area** counts the square units a shape covers; **perimeter** is the distance around its edge. Area is measured in square units (like $\text{cm}^2$).

A **parallelogram**'s area is **base × height**. The **height** is the perpendicular distance between the two bases — NOT the length of the slanted side. (Shear a rectangle sideways and its area doesn't change.)
A **triangle** is exactly **half** of a parallelogram with the same base and height, so its area is **$\tfrac12 \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$**.
Always measure the **height perpendicular** to the base you chose.
lessons.keyIdea: Parallelogram area = base × height; triangle area = ½ × base × height. The height is the perpendicular distance to the base, never the slanted side.

workedExamples.statement: A parallelogram has base $8$ and height $5$. Find its area.
A triangle has base $10$ and height $6$. Find its area.

commonMistakes.text: Using the slanted side as the height.
commonMistakes.correction: The **height is perpendicular** to the base — the straight-across distance, not the tilted edge.
A triangle is **half** its base-height rectangle. Always include the $\tfrac12$.

steps.title: Area vs perimeter
beats: **Area** = the square units a shape covers (measured in $\text{cm}^2$, $\text{m}^2$…).
**Perimeter** = the distance around the outside.
A rectangle's area is length × width.
Push a rectangle over into a **parallelogram** — the area stays the same.

steps.title: Parallelogram: base × height
steps.teach: Change the base and height. The area is always base × height — and the **height** is the straight-up perpendicular distance (the dashed line), not the slanted side. A $8 \times 5$ parallelogram has area $40$.

steps.title: Base times perpendicular height
beats: $A = b \times h$.
$b$ is the base; $h$ is the **perpendicular** height to it.
The tilted side is longer than $h$ — don't use it.
Base $8$, height $5$: $A = 40$.

problems.prompt: The height of a parallelogram is measured —

steps.title: Triangle: half of that
steps.teach: A triangle is exactly half a parallelogram with the same base and height. So its area is $\tfrac12 \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$. Base $8$, height $5$ gives $\tfrac12(40) = 20$.

steps.title: Half the base-height rectangle
beats: Two copies of a triangle make a parallelogram.
So the triangle is **half**: $A = \tfrac12 bh$.
Base $10$, height $6$: $\tfrac12(60) = 30$.
Still use the **perpendicular** height.

problems.prompt: A triangle's area is — a parallelogram with the same base and height.

steps.title: Why surveyors split land into triangles
steps.body: Land surveyors split any oddly-shaped plot into triangles and add their areas — because a triangle's area needs only a base and a height, both easy to measure in the field. Break a shape into triangles and you can find the area of almost anything.

steps.title: Triangle area
steps.prompt: A triangle has base $12$ and height $9$. What is its area?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Area** counts square units inside; **perimeter** is the distance around.
The **height is perpendicular** to the base — never the slanted side.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find parallelogram and triangle areas with base and perpendicular height.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The height of a triangle must be —
A triangle with base $10$, height $10$. Area?

steps.title: You've finished "Area of Parallelograms & Triangles"
steps.body: You can find parallelogram and triangle areas. Next: trapezoids (average the bases) and rhombi and kites (half the diagonals).

lessons.title: Area of Trapezoids, Rhombi & Kites
lessons.concreteComparison: A trapezoid has a wide base and a narrower top — so you average the two before multiplying by the height. A rhombus or kite is measured a totally different way: half the product of its two diagonals.
lessons.objective: Find the area of a trapezoid using ½(b₁+b₂)·h and the area of a rhombus or kite using ½·d₁·d₂.
concept: A **trapezoid** has two parallel bases $b_1$ and $b_2$. Its area is the **average of the bases times the height**: $A = \tfrac12(b_1 + b_2)h$.
A **rhombus** or **kite** has **perpendicular diagonals**; its area is **half the product of the diagonals**: $A = \tfrac12 d_1 d_2$.
The trapezoid formula reuses base and perpendicular height; the diagonal formula is a different route that fits shapes with crossing diagonals.
If the two bases of a trapezoid are equal, it's a parallelogram — and $\tfrac12(b + b)h = bh$ agrees.
lessons.keyIdea: Trapezoid area = ½(b₁+b₂)·h (average base × height). Rhombus/kite area = ½·d₁·d₂ (half the product of the diagonals).

facts.title: Trapezoid
facts.explanation: Average of the bases times the height.

facts.title: Rhombus / kite
facts.explanation: Half the product of the diagonals.

workedExamples.statement: A trapezoid has bases $6$ and $10$ and height $4$. Find its area.
A rhombus has diagonals $8$ and $6$. Find its area.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to average the two bases.
commonMistakes.correction: A trapezoid uses $\tfrac12(b_1 + b_2)$ — the **average** of the two bases, not just one.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying the full diagonals for a rhombus.
commonMistakes.correction: It's **half** the product: $\tfrac12 d_1 d_2$. Don't skip the $\tfrac12$.

steps.title: Two more area recipes
beats: A **trapezoid** has two parallel bases of different lengths.
Average them, then multiply by the height.
A **rhombus** or **kite** has crossing perpendicular diagonals.
Its area comes from those diagonals instead.

steps.title: Trapezoid: average the bases
steps.teach: Change the bottom base, top base, and height. The area is $\tfrac12(b_1 + b_2)h$ — the **average** of the two bases times the height. Bases $8$ and $4$, height $5$: $\tfrac12(12)(5) = 30$.

steps.title: Average base × height
beats: Add the two parallel bases, halve, times the height.
Bases $6$ and $10$, height $4$: $\tfrac12(16)(4) = 32$.
Equal bases give a parallelogram: $\tfrac12(b + b)h = bh$.

problems.prompt: A trapezoid's area uses the — of the two bases.

steps.title: Rhombus & kite: half the diagonals
steps.teach: A rhombus (and a kite) has perpendicular diagonals. Its area is $\tfrac12 d_1 d_2$ — half the product of the two diagonals. Diagonals $8$ and $6$ give $\tfrac12(48) = 24$.

steps.title: Half the product of diagonals
beats: The diagonals cross at right angles.
$A = \tfrac12 d_1 d_2$.
Diagonals $8$ and $6$: $\tfrac12(48) = 24$.
The same formula works for a **kite**.

steps.title: Rhombus / kite area
steps.intro: Half the product of the diagonals.

problems.prompt: The rhombus area formula uses the two —
problems.explanation: Half the product of the diagonals.

steps.title: Why land was once measured by diagonals
steps.body: Surveyors of irregular four-sided plots often measured the two diagonals rather than wrestle with slanted sides — because for kite- and rhombus-shaped fields the half-the-diagonals formula gives the area directly. It's faster than dropping perpendiculars in a muddy field.

steps.title: Trapezoid area
steps.prompt: A trapezoid has bases $7$ and $13$ and height $4$. What is its area?

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Trapezoid**: $A = \tfrac12(b_1 + b_2)h$ — average the bases, times the height.
**Rhombus / kite**: $A = \tfrac12 d_1 d_2$ — half the product of the diagonals.
Equal bases turn the trapezoid formula into $bh$ (a parallelogram).

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's average bases for trapezoids and multiply diagonals for rhombi and kites.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A trapezoid with equal bases $b$ is a parallelogram with area —

steps.title: You've finished "Area of Trapezoids, Rhombi & Kites"
steps.body: You can find areas of the trickier quadrilaterals. Next: regular polygons — split into triangles from the center — using the apothem.

lessons.title: Area of Regular Polygons
lessons.concreteComparison: Slice a regular hexagon from its center and you get six identical triangles, like a pie. Add up their areas and the whole thing comes out to half the apothem times the perimeter — one formula for any regular polygon.
lessons.objective: Find the area of a regular polygon using ½ × apothem × perimeter.
concept: The **apothem** ($a$) is the perpendicular distance from the center of a regular polygon to the middle of a side.
Split a regular $n$-gon into **$n$ triangles** from the center. Each triangle has base = one **side** and height = the **apothem**.
Adding them: $A = n \times \tfrac12(\text{side})(a) = \tfrac12 \, a \times (n \cdot \text{side}) = \tfrac12 \, a \, P$, where $P$ is the **perimeter**.
So **$A = \tfrac12 a P$** — half the apothem times the perimeter.
lessons.keyIdea: A regular polygon splits into n triangles from the center, each with base = side and height = apothem. Total area = ½ · apothem · perimeter.

facts.title: Apothem
facts.explanation: The perpendicular distance from center to a side.

facts.title: Regular polygon area
facts.explanation: Half the apothem times the perimeter.

workedExamples.statement: A regular polygon has apothem $6$ and perimeter $40$. Find its area.
A regular hexagon has side $10$ and apothem $9$ (rounded). Find its area.

commonMistakes.text: Using a full side or a diagonal instead of the apothem.
commonMistakes.correction: The height of each triangle is the **apothem** — center to the **middle of a side**, at a right angle.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to find the perimeter first.
commonMistakes.correction: $P = n \times \text{side}$. Multiply the side by the number of sides before using $\tfrac12 aP$.

steps.title: Slice it into triangles
beats: A **regular polygon** has equal sides and equal angles.
Draw lines from the center to each vertex — you get **$n$ triangles**.
Each triangle's base is a **side**; its height is the **apothem** (the distance from the center to the middle of a side).
Add them all up for the total area.

steps.title: n triangles, height = apothem
steps.teach: Change the number of sides. The polygon splits into $n$ equal triangles from the center; the **apothem** (blue, meeting the side at a right angle) is each triangle's height. The total area is $\tfrac12 \times \text{apothem} \times \text{perimeter}$.

steps.title: Center to the middle of a side
beats: The **apothem** $a$ runs from the center to the **midpoint** of a side.
It meets the side at a **right angle**.
It's the height of each little triangle.
Not to be confused with the radius (center to a vertex).

problems.prompt: The apothem goes from the center to the —

steps.title: P = n × side
beats: Often you're given the **side** and the **apothem**.
First find the perimeter: $P = n \times \text{side}$.
Then $A = \tfrac12 a P$.
Hexagon, side $10$: $P = 60$.

steps.title: From side & apothem
steps.intro: Perimeter first, then ½ a P.

steps.title: Side & apothem
steps.intro: P = n·side, then ½ a P.

problems.prompt: For a hexagon, the perimeter is — the side.

steps.title: Why bubbles and beehives are hexagons
steps.body: Among all regular polygons that tile the plane, the hexagon encloses the most area for the least perimeter — so bees building comb and soap bubbles packing together both settle into hexagons to save wax and surface. The apothem-and-perimeter area formula is exactly what makes that efficiency measurable.

steps.title: Polygon area
steps.prompt: A regular polygon has apothem $7$ and perimeter $40$. What is its area?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A regular $n$-gon splits into **$n$ triangles** from the center.
Each has base = **side**, height = **apothem** (center to a side's midpoint).
**Area $= \tfrac12 a P$** — half the apothem times the perimeter ($P = n \times$ side).

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's use ½ · apothem · perimeter, finding the perimeter first when needed.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: The apothem is the — of each of the n triangles.
For a square of side $s$, the perimeter is —

steps.title: You've finished "Area of Regular Polygons"
steps.body: You can find any regular polygon's area with ½ a P. Next: circles — circumference, area, and the sector slice.

lessons.title: Circumference, Circle Area & Sectors
lessons.concreteComparison: The distance around a circle is its **circumference**; the space inside is its **area**. A pizza slice — a **sector** — is just a fraction of each. Every circle measurement flows from $\pi$, the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
lessons.objective: Find the circumference (2πr), the area of a circle (πr²), and the area of a sector ((θ/360)·πr²), leaving answers in terms of π.
concept: **Circumference** (the distance around) $= 2\pi r = \pi d$.
**Area of a circle** (the space inside) $= \pi r^2$.
A **sector** is a pie slice — the fraction $\tfrac{\theta}{360}$ of the whole circle. **Sector area** $= \tfrac{\theta}{360}\,\pi r^2$.
Answers are usually left **in terms of $\pi$** (like $16\pi$) unless a decimal is asked for.
lessons.keyIdea: Circumference = 2πr; circle area = πr². A sector is the θ/360 fraction of the circle, so its area is (θ/360)·πr².

workedExamples.statement: A circle has radius $5$. Find its circumference and area (in terms of π).
A $90^\circ$ sector of a circle of radius $8$. Find its area.

commonMistakes.text: Using the diameter as $r$ in the area formula.
commonMistakes.correction: The area uses the **radius**: $\pi r^2$. If given the diameter, halve it first.
**Circumference** is $2\pi r$ (no square); only the **area** squares the radius.

steps.title: Around and inside
beats: **Circumference** is the distance around a circle: $C = 2\pi r$.
**Area** is the space inside: $A = \pi r^2$.
$\pi \approx 3.14$, the ratio of circumference to diameter.
A **sector** is a slice — a fraction of the whole.

steps.title: The sector is a fraction
steps.teach: A sector is the fraction $\tfrac{\theta}{360}$ of the circle. Change $\theta$: the arc length is that fraction of the circumference $2\pi r$, and the sector area is that fraction of the area $\pi r^2$. At $90^\circ$ both are $\tfrac14$.

steps.title: Two formulas
beats: **Circumference** $= 2\pi r$ (or $\pi d$).
**Area** $= \pi r^2$ — the radius is **squared**.
Given a diameter? Halve it to get $r$ first.
Leave answers as multiples of $\pi$ unless told otherwise.

steps.title: A fraction of the circle
beats: A **sector** is the fraction $\tfrac{\theta}{360}$ of the circle.
**Sector area** $= \tfrac{\theta}{360}\,\pi r^2$.
A semicircle ($180^\circ$) is half; a quarter ($90^\circ$) is a fourth.

problems.prompt: A $60^\circ$ sector is — of the circle.

steps.title: How π sneaks into everything round
steps.body: Because area is $\pi r^2$, doubling a pizza's radius quadruples the food — a $16$-inch pizza has four times the area of an $8$-inch one, not twice. That's why the big pizza is almost always the better deal per dollar: area grows with the square of the radius.

steps.title: Circle area
steps.prompt: A circle has radius $7$. What is its area (in terms of π)?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **sector** is the fraction $\tfrac{\theta}{360}$ of the circle: area $= \tfrac{\theta}{360}\pi r^2$.
Leave answers **in terms of $\pi$** unless a decimal is asked for.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's find circumferences, circle areas, and sector areas in terms of π.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

steps.title: You've finished "Circumference, Circle Area & Sectors"
steps.body: You can measure any circle and its slices. Next: composite figures — shapes built from several simple pieces added together.

lessons.title: Composite Figures
lessons.concreteComparison: A house-shaped sign is a rectangle with a triangle roof; an L-shaped garden is two rectangles; a running track is a rectangle with two semicircle ends. Break a complex figure into simple shapes, find each area, and add them up.
lessons.objective: Find the area of a composite figure by decomposing it into rectangles, triangles, and semicircles, then adding the parts.
concept: A **composite figure** is made of several simple shapes joined together.
**Decompose** it into rectangles, triangles, semicircles, and so on.
Find each piece's area with its own formula.
**Add** the pieces to get the total area. (Watch which lengths belong to which piece.)
lessons.keyIdea: To find a composite area, split the figure into simple shapes, find each area, and add them up.

facts.title: Add the parts
facts.explanation: Combine the pieces for the whole.

workedExamples.statement: A figure is a rectangle $12 \times 5$ with a triangle (base $12$, height $3$) on top. Find the total area.
An L-shape is a $6 \times 4$ rectangle plus a $3 \times 2$ rectangle. Find the total area.

commonMistakes.correction: Split cleanly so pieces don't overlap; use each length for exactly **one** piece.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing up a piece's own base and height.
commonMistakes.correction: Use the dimensions that belong to **that piece** — a triangle's height isn't the whole figure's height.

tryIt.statement: Rectangle $10 \times 4$ plus triangle base $10$ height $6$. Total area?

steps.title: Break it into simple shapes
beats: A **composite figure** is several simple shapes stuck together.
**Decompose** it — draw lines to split it into rectangles and triangles.
Find each piece's area on its own.
**Add** them for the total.

steps.title: Rectangle + triangle
steps.teach: This 'house' splits into a rectangle ($12 \times 5 = 60$) and a triangle on top (base $12$, height $3$, area $18$). Add them: $60 + 18 = 78$.

steps.title: One piece at a time
beats: Draw a line to cut the figure into shapes you know.
Find each area with its formula.
Keep track of which length belongs to which piece.
**Add** all the piece areas.

examples.prompt: Rectangle $12 \times 5$ with a triangle (base $12$, height $3$) on top. Total?

problems.prompt: A composite figure's area is the — of its pieces.

steps.title: Mixing straight and round
beats: Some figures add a **semicircle** to a rectangle (a window, a track end).
Rectangle area is straightforward.
Semicircle area is **half** a circle: $\tfrac12 \pi r^2$.
Add them — often left in terms of $\pi$.

examples.prompt: Rectangle $10 \times 4$ with a semicircle (radius $2$) on one end. Total?
Rectangle $8 \times 6$ with a semicircle (radius $3$) on top. Total?

problems.prompt: A semicircle's area is — a full circle.

steps.title: Every floor plan is a composite figure
steps.body: Architects and flooring installers compute room areas exactly this way — splitting an irregular floor into rectangles and triangles, finding each, and adding. It's why a quote for tiling an L-shaped kitchen comes back as the sum of two rectangle areas.

steps.title: Total area
steps.prompt: A figure is a $9 \times 4$ rectangle with a triangle (base $9$, height $6$) on top. What is the total area?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **composite figure** is made of simple shapes joined together.
**Decompose** into rectangles, triangles, semicircles; find each area.
**Add** the parts for the total (semicircle $= \tfrac12\pi r^2$).

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's decompose figures and add up the pieces.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: A composite figure is best found by —

steps.title: You've finished "Composite Figures"
steps.body: You can add up areas of combined shapes. Last stop: shaded regions — where you SUBTRACT one area from another.

lessons.title: Shaded Regions & Applications
lessons.concreteComparison: A round rug on a square floor, a washer (a metal ring), a picture inside a frame — the region you want is often what's LEFT after removing a smaller shape from a bigger one. Instead of adding, you subtract.
lessons.objective: Find the area of a shaded region by subtracting an inner shape from an outer one, including rings (annuli), and apply area to real problems.
concept: A **shaded region** is often a larger shape with a smaller shape **removed** from it.
Find the **outer** area and the **inner** area, then **subtract**: shaded $=$ outer $-$ inner.
A **ring (annulus)** is a big circle minus a small circle: $\pi R^2 - \pi r^2$.
Real problems — flooring left after a rug, a path around a pool, a matted photo — are area subtractions.
lessons.keyIdea: A shaded region = outer area − inner area. A ring is πR² − πr². Real 'leftover' area problems are subtractions.

workedExamples.statement: A square of side $10$ has a circle of radius $3$ cut out. Find the shaded area (in terms of π).
A ring has outer radius $5$ and inner radius $3$. Find its area (in terms of π).

commonMistakes.correction: For a **removed** shape, subtract: shaded $=$ outer $-$ inner. Only combine (add) when both pieces are kept.

commonMistakes.text: Subtracting the radii instead of the areas.
commonMistakes.correction: For a ring, subtract the **areas**: $\pi R^2 - \pi r^2$, not $\pi(R - r)^2$.

tryIt.statement: Square side $8$ with a circle radius $2$ removed. Shaded area?
Ring, outer radius $6$, inner radius $4$. Area?

steps.title: What's left after removing
beats: A **shaded region** is often a big shape with a hole cut out.
Find the **outer** area.
Find the **inner** (removed) area.
**Subtract**: shaded $=$ outer $-$ inner.

steps.title: Square minus a circle
steps.teach: The shaded region is a $10 \times 10$ square ($100$) with a circle of radius $3$ removed ($9\pi$). Subtract: $100 - 9\pi$. The removed circle is drawn hollow (dashed).

steps.title: Outer minus inner
beats: Compute the outer shape's area.
Compute the removed shape's area.
**Shaded $=$ outer $-$ inner.**
Leave $\pi$ in the answer if a circle is involved.

examples.prompt: Square side $10$, circle radius $3$ removed. Shaded?
Rectangle $12 \times 8$ with a $4 \times 3$ rectangle removed. Shaded?

problems.prompt: Square side $8$, circle radius $2$ removed. Shaded?
For a removed shape you — its area.

steps.title: A circle with a circular hole
beats: A **ring** is a big circle with a smaller circle removed.
Outer area $= \pi R^2$; inner area $= \pi r^2$.
**Ring area $= \pi R^2 - \pi r^2$.**
Subtract the **areas**, not the radii.

examples.prompt: Outer radius $5$, inner radius $3$. Ring area?
Outer radius $6$, inner radius $4$. Ring area?

problems.prompt: Outer radius $4$, inner radius $2$. Ring area?
Outer radius $10$, inner radius $6$. Ring area?
Which is the CORRECT area formula for a ring (outer radius $R$, inner radius $r$)?
problems.explanation: Subtract the two circle AREAS: $\pi R^2 - \pi r^2$ — not $\pi(R-r)^2$.

steps.title: Why a washer works
steps.body: A metal washer is a real annulus — its material area is $\pi R^2 - \pi r^2$, the big disk minus the bolt hole. Engineers use exactly this subtraction to compute how much metal a washer contains and how much pressure it can spread under a bolt.

steps.title: Shaded area
steps.prompt: A square of side $6$ has a circle of radius $2$ removed. What is the shaded area (in terms of π)?

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **shaded region** $=$ **outer area − inner area** (the removed shape).
A **ring (annulus)** $= \pi R^2 - \pi r^2$ — subtract the areas, not the radii.
Real 'leftover' problems (rugs, paths, washers) are area subtractions.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Let's subtract inner shapes and compute rings.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

Ring, outer radius $7$, inner radius $5$. Area?
For a shape that is REMOVED, you —

steps.title: You've finished "Shaded Regions & Applications"
steps.body: You can find leftover and ring areas by subtracting — the finale of Area & Perimeter. Next unit: surface area and volume, where these area skills wrap and fill 3-D solids.
